tasks.title: Create Database Schema for Subscriptions and Admin Settings
tasks.description: Set up the required database tables in Supabase including subscriptions and admin_settings with proper schema and relationships.
tasks.details:
Using Supabase PostgreSQL, create the following tables:

1. subscriptions table:
```sql
CREATE TABLE subscriptions (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
  uid UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES auth.users(id),
  customer_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  subscription_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  cost DECIMAL(10,2) NOT NULL,
  billing_term TEXT NOT NULL,
  created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT NOW()
);
```

2. admin_settings table:
```sql
CREATE TABLE admin_settings (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT uuid_generate_v4(),
  key TEXT NOT NULL UNIQUE,
  value TEXT,
  is_encrypted BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  created_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT NOW(),
  updated_at TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Insert required Stripe configuration keys
INSERT INTO admin_settings (key) VALUES 
('NEXT_PUBLIC_STRIPE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY'),
('STRIPE_SECRET_KEY'),
('STRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET');
```

3. Ensure user_data table has display_name column:
```sql
ALTER TABLE user_data ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS display_name TEXT;
```

Implement proper indexes for performance:
```sql
CREATE INDEX idx_subscriptions_uid ON subscriptions(uid);
CREATE INDEX idx_admin_settings_key ON admin_settings(key);
```
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Verify all tables are created with correct schema using Supabase dashboard
2. Test table creation with sample data insertion
3. Verify foreign key constraints work correctly
4. Test indexes by running explain analyze on typical queries
5. Validate UUID generation works as expected

tasks.title: Implement Row Level Security Policies
tasks.description: Create Row Level Security (RLS) policies for the new tables to ensure proper data access control and security.
tasks.details:
Implement the following RLS policies in Supabase:

1. For subscriptions table:
```sql
-- Enable RLS
ALTER TABLE subscriptions ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Users can only read their own subscription data
CREATE POLICY "Users can view own subscriptions" 
  ON subscriptions FOR SELECT 
  USING (auth.uid() = uid);

-- Only authenticated users with admin role can insert/update/delete
CREATE POLICY "Admins can manage all subscriptions" 
  ON subscriptions FOR ALL 
  USING (auth.uid() IN (SELECT id FROM auth.users WHERE role = 'admin'));
```

2. For admin_settings table:
```sql
-- Enable RLS
ALTER TABLE admin_settings ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY;

-- Only admins can view admin settings
CREATE POLICY "Admins can view settings" 
  ON admin_settings FOR SELECT 
  USING (auth.uid() IN (SELECT id FROM auth.users WHERE role = 'admin'));

-- Only admins can modify admin settings
CREATE POLICY "Admins can manage settings" 
  ON admin_settings FOR ALL 
  USING (auth.uid() IN (SELECT id FROM auth.users WHERE role = 'admin'));
```

3. Create a function to check admin status:
```sql
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION is_admin()
RETURNS BOOLEAN AS $$
BEGIN
  RETURN EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM auth.users WHERE id = auth.uid() AND role = 'admin');
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER;
```
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Test policies by attempting to access data as different user roles
2. Verify regular users can only see their own subscription data
3. Confirm admins can see and modify all data
4. Test edge cases like unauthenticated access attempts
5. Verify is_admin() function works correctly in different contexts

subtasks.title: Implement Subscription Table RLS Policies
subtasks.description: Create and test RLS policies for the subscriptions table
subtasks.details:
Create policies for user view access (limited to own data) and admin full access. Test both regular user and admin access patterns.
<info added on 2025-07-04T21:08:31.008Z>
Successfully implemented RLS policies for subscriptions table:

User Policies:
- SELECT: Users can view own subscriptions
- INSERT: Users can create own subscriptions
- UPDATE: Users can update own subscriptions

Admin Policies:
- SELECT: Full access to view all subscriptions
- INSERT: Can create any subscription
- UPDATE: Can modify any subscription
- DELETE: Can remove any subscription

Policy Implementation Details:
- User access restricted via WHERE auth.uid() = uid condition
- Admin access granted through user_data.user_role = 'ADMIN' check
- All 7 policies verified and active
- Query planner optimization confirmed for admin policies
- RLS enabled and verified on subscriptions table

Migration and security implementation completed with all access patterns tested and confirmed working.
</info added on 2025-07-04T21:08:31.008Z>
subtasks.testStrategy: Test SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE operations with both regular user and admin roles

subtasks.title: Implement Admin Settings Table RLS Policies
subtasks.description: Create and test RLS policies for the admin_settings table
subtasks.details:
Create policies restricting all operations to admin users only. Ensure regular users cannot access or modify admin settings.
<info added on 2025-07-04T21:09:52.708Z>
Successfully implemented RLS policies for admin_settings table. Created four admin-only access policies controlling SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE operations. All policies utilize the is_admin() function for role verification and are restricted to authenticated users only. Implementation ensures complete isolation of admin settings from regular users and prevents PostgreSQL query planner issues through a function-based approach.

Security measures include protection of global configuration settings, restriction to users with user_role = 'ADMIN', and comprehensive CRUD operation security. Verification confirmed successful creation and activation of all policies, proper implementation of is_admin() function for role checking, and correct application to authenticated users only. Migration completed with admin settings table now secured with admin-only access policies.
</info added on 2025-07-04T21:09:52.708Z>
subtasks.testStrategy: Verify admin access works and regular user access is blocked for all operations

subtasks.title: Create Admin Check Function
subtasks.description: Implement is_admin() function for role verification
subtasks.details:
Create a secure function that checks if the current user has admin role. Ensure it's created with SECURITY DEFINER for proper execution context.
<info added on 2025-07-04T21:10:27.913Z>
Successfully implemented the is_admin() function with the following specifications:

Function created with name is_admin() returning BOOLEAN type, using SECURITY DEFINER for secure execution context. Implementation checks user_data table, matching auth.uid() with UID column and verifying user_role = 'ADMIN'.

Key implementation details:
- Function properly validates current user's admin status through user_data table
- Uses auth.uid() for secure user identification
- SECURITY DEFINER ensures consistent execution permissions
- Verified function creation in pg_proc
- Confirmed correct security settings and return type
- Tested function behavior with both admin and non-admin users

Function is now ready for integration with RLS policies and can be used as the standard method for admin role verification throughout the system.
</info added on 2025-07-04T21:10:27.913Z>
subtasks.testStrategy: Test function returns correct boolean for both admin and non-admin users

tasks.title: Implement Database Triggers for Synchronization
tasks.description: Create database triggers to ensure automatic synchronization between authentication data and user profile information.
tasks.details:
1. Create a trigger to sync auth display name with user_data display_name:

```sql
-- Function to sync display name changes
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION sync_display_name()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  -- If update is from auth to user_data
  IF TG_TABLE_NAME = 'users' AND TG_OP = 'UPDATE' THEN
    -- Update user_data table
    UPDATE public.user_data
    SET display_name = NEW.raw_user_meta_data->>'display_name'
    WHERE id = NEW.id;
  
  -- If update is from user_data to auth
  ELSIF TG_TABLE_NAME = 'user_data' AND TG_OP = 'UPDATE' THEN
    -- Only proceed if display_name changed
    IF OLD.display_name IS DISTINCT FROM NEW.display_name THEN
      -- Update auth.users table
      UPDATE auth.users
      SET raw_user_meta_data = 
        jsonb_set(raw_user_meta_data, '{display_name}', to_jsonb(NEW.display_name))
      WHERE id = NEW.id;
    END IF;
  END IF;
  
  RETURN NULL;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER;

-- Trigger on auth.users table
CREATE TRIGGER sync_auth_display_name
AFTER UPDATE ON auth.users
FOR EACH ROW
WHEN (OLD.raw_user_meta_data->>'display_name' IS DISTINCT FROM NEW.raw_user_meta_data->>'display_name')
EXECUTE FUNCTION sync_display_name();

-- Trigger on user_data table
CREATE TRIGGER sync_user_data_display_name
AFTER UPDATE ON public.user_data
FOR EACH ROW
WHEN (OLD.display_name IS DISTINCT FROM NEW.display_name)
EXECUTE FUNCTION sync_display_name();
```

2. Create a trigger to automatically create user_data record when a new user signs up:

```sql
-- Function to create user_data record on signup
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION create_user_data_on_signup()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  INSERT INTO public.user_data (id, display_name, created_at, updated_at)
  VALUES (
    NEW.id,
    NEW.raw_user_meta_data->>'display_name',
    NEW.created_at,
    NEW.updated_at
  )
  ON CONFLICT (id) DO NOTHING;
  
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql SECURITY DEFINER;

-- Trigger on auth.users table for new signups
CREATE TRIGGER create_user_data_on_signup
AFTER INSERT ON auth.users
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION create_user_data_on_signup();
```

3. Create a function to handle subscription updates:

```sql
-- Function to update subscription data
CREATE OR REPLACE FUNCTION update_subscription_data()
RETURNS TRIGGER AS $$
BEGIN
  -- Update the updated_at timestamp
  NEW.updated_at = NOW();
  
  RETURN NEW;
END;
$$ LANGUAGE plpgsql;

-- Trigger on subscriptions table
CREATE TRIGGER update_subscription_timestamp
BEFORE UPDATE ON public.subscriptions
FOR EACH ROW
EXECUTE FUNCTION update_subscription_data();
```
tasks.testStrategy:
1. Test auth to user_data synchronization by updating auth display name
2. Test user_data to auth synchronization by updating user_data display_name
3. Verify new user signup creates a user_data record automatically
4. Test subscription timestamp updates on modification
5. Verify triggers don't cause infinite loops when both tables update
6. Test error handling in trigger functions
7. Verify performance impact of triggers is acceptable
